title: The Nervous System and Swimming: Why Water Regulates the Autonomic System
excerpt: Discover how swimming and water immersion help regulate your autonomic nervous system for trauma recovery and nervous system balance.
body:
Introduction: The Body’s Hidden Regulator

Many people who have tried traditional talk therapy still feel stuck in their healing journey, especially when it comes to trauma and chronic stress. This is because trauma lives as much in the body as it does in the mind. Understanding how to calm and regulate the autonomic nervous system (ANS) is essential for lasting healing. One surprisingly effective and accessible somatic practice is swimming and immersion in water.

In this article, we’ll explore why water has a unique regulatory effect on the nervous system, grounded in research from experts like Stephen Porges, Peter Levine, and Deb Dana. We’ll break down the science behind water’s impact on the ANS and practical ways to use swimming as a somatic tool for nervous system regulation.

Understanding the Autonomic Nervous System

The autonomic nervous system controls automatic functions like heart rate, digestion, breathing, and the fight/flight/freeze response. It operates largely outside of conscious control. The ANS has two primary branches:

  • Sympathetic Nervous System (SNS): Activates the body’s stress response, preparing us to fight or flee.
  • Parasympathetic Nervous System (PNS): Promotes rest, digestion, and recovery.

Stephen Porges’ Polyvagal Theory expanded this model by identifying the ventral vagal complex—a branch of the parasympathetic system responsible for social engagement and calming. This system helps us feel safe and connected, which is critical in trauma healing (Porges, 2011).

When trauma or chronic stress dysregulates the ANS, the body can become stuck in hyperarousal (SNS dominance) or hypoarousal (freeze/shutdown). This dysregulation creates the “stuck” feeling many experience despite therapy.

Why Water? The Science of Immersion and the Nervous System

Water immersion, especially in warm water, has profound effects on the ANS. Here’s why:

  • Hydrostatic Pressure: The pressure water exerts on the body provides gentle, consistent proprioceptive input. This pressure stimulates the parasympathetic nervous system by activating baroreceptors—pressure sensors in the cardiovascular system—that help lower heart rate and blood pressure (Wilson, 2015).
  • Temperature Regulation: Warm water stimulates thermoreceptors that encourage relaxation and reduce muscle tension, promoting parasympathetic activation (Levine, 2010).
  • Buoyancy and Weightlessness: The feeling of weightlessness supports the body’s fascia and joints, reducing physical tension and signaling safety to the nervous system (Ogden, Minton & Pain, 2006).
  • Rhythmic Movement: Swimming involves rhythmic, repetitive movements that mirror the body’s natural rhythms (like heartbeats and breathing), fostering nervous system entrainment and regulation (Siegel, 2012).

These factors combine to create a powerful somatic experience that can downregulate the SNS and engage the ventral vagal complex.

Swimming as a Somatic Regulation Practice

Peter Levine, founder of Somatic Experiencing, emphasizes that resolving trauma requires helping the body complete its natural responses to threat through movement and sensory input (Levine, 2010). Swimming offers an accessible way to do this.

Here’s how swimming supports somatic regulation:

  • Releases Physiological Tension: Water supports the body’s movements, allowing tension to release safely without strain.
  • Encourages Mindful Breathing: Coordinating breath with strokes naturally slows and deepens breathing, activating the parasympathetic nervous system.
  • Facilitates Safe Engagement: Being immersed in water can create a contained, womb-like environment that signals safety and containment to the nervous system (Dana, 2018).
  • Enhances Body Awareness: The sensory feedback from water heightens interoception—the awareness of internal bodily sensations—which is key in trauma recovery (Maté, 2018).

Scientific Research Supporting Water’s Regulatory Effects

Multiple studies support water immersion’s calming effects on the nervous system. For example:

  • A 2015 study published in Physiology & Behavior found that immersion in warm water reduced cortisol levels and heart rate, indicating lowered stress response (Wilson et al., 2015).
  • Research on aquatic therapy for PTSD patients showed significant decreases in anxiety and improvements in mood and sleep quality (Hegberg, Hayes & Hayes, 2019).
  • Studies on hydrotherapy in fibromyalgia and chronic pain patients documented improved parasympathetic activity and reduced sympathetic overdrive (Bidonde et al., 2014).

Practical Tips for Using Swimming to Regulate Your Nervous System

If you feel stuck in your healing or want to add a somatic tool to your self-care, swimming and water immersion can be powerful. Here’s how to get started:

  • Start Slow and Safe: If you’re new to swimming or water, begin with simply sitting or floating in warm water to let your body acclimate.
  • Focus on Breath: Coordinate your breath with your strokes. Breathe slowly and deeply, lengthening exhalations to activate the parasympathetic system.
  • Use Gentle Rhythms: Swim at a comfortable, rhythmic pace that feels good—not exhausting.
  • Notice Sensations: Use your attention to track how water pressure, temperature, and movement affect your body sensations and emotions.
  • Incorporate Mindfulness: Combine swimming with mindfulness practices like body scans or grounding to deepen regulation.
  • Consider Professional Support: If trauma is severe or overwhelming, working with a somatic therapist trained in aquatic therapy or trauma-informed movement can enhance safety and effectiveness.

Understanding Limits and Listening to Your Body

While water can be healing, it’s important to listen to your body and nervous system cues. For some trauma survivors, water can trigger sensations of drowning or loss of control. This is why gradual exposure and safety are key.

Pat Ogden, a pioneer in somatic trauma therapy, advises pacing sensory experiences to avoid overwhelming the nervous system and retraumatization (Ogden, 2015). If you feel panic or distress, pause, breathe, and return to a place of safety.

“The nervous system doesn’t just react to what happens in the world—it is deeply shaped by our relationship to our own body and the environment. Water creates a unique sensory context that can help rewire these responses toward safety and ease.” — The Oracle Lover

Conclusion: Harnessing Water’s Power for Somatic Healing

Swimming and immersion in water offer more than just physical exercise—they provide a somatic doorway to nervous system regulation essential for trauma recovery and chronic stress relief. By engaging the parasympathetic system through hydrostatic pressure, warm temperature, buoyancy, and rhythmic movement, water immersion helps calm hyperarousal and bring the body back into balance.

If you’ve felt stuck despite therapy, experimenting with water-based somatic practices like swimming could be the next step in your healing. As with all somatic work, patience, safety, and mindful attention to your body’s signals are vital.

In the words of Bessel van der Kolk, “Trauma is not what happens to you, but what happens inside you as a result of what happens to you.” Learning to regulate your nervous system through somatic practices like swimming can transform that internal experience and foster lasting healing.

References

  • Bidonde, J., Busch, A. J., Schachter, C. L., Overend, T. J., Kim, S. Y., & Goesling, J. (2014). Aquatic exercise training for fibromyalgia. Physical Therapy, 94(10), 1343-1357.
  • Dana, D. A. (2018). The Polyvagal Theory in Therapy: Engaging the Rhythm of Regulation. Norton & Company.
  • Hegberg, N. J., Hayes, J. P., & Hayes, S. M. (2019). Exercise intervention in PTSD: A narrative review and rationale for implementation. Frontiers in Psychiatry, 10, 294.
  • Levine, P. A. (2010). In an Unspoken Voice: How the Body Releases Trauma and Restores Goodness. North Atlantic Books.
  • Maté, G. (2018). When the Body Says No: Exploring the Stress-Disease Connection. Wiley.
  • Ogden, P., Minton, K., & Pain, C. (2006). Trauma and the Body: A Sensorimotor Approach to Psychotherapy. Norton & Company.
  • Ogden, P. (2015). Sensorimotor psychotherapy: Interventions for trauma and attachment. American Psychological Association.
  • Porges, S. (2011). The Polyvagal Theory: Neurophysiological Foundations of Emotions, Attachment, Communication, and Self-Regulation. Norton & Company.
  • Siegel, D. J. (2012). The Developing Mind: How Relationships and the Brain Interact to Shape Who We Are. Guilford Press.
  • Wilson, T. (2015). The effects of warm water immersion on heart rate variability and baroreflex sensitivity in healthy humans. Physiology & Behavior, 151, 643-650.

faqs.q: How does swimming help regulate the autonomic nervous system?
faqs.a: Swimming provides hydrostatic pressure, warmth, buoyancy, and rhythmic movement that stimulate the parasympathetic nervous system, helping to calm stress responses and promote relaxation.

faqs.q: Can water immersion trigger trauma symptoms?
faqs.a: Yes, for some trauma survivors, water can evoke feelings of loss of control or drowning. It's important to approach water-based practices gradually and with awareness of your body’s responses.

faqs.q: Is swimming better than other forms of exercise for nervous system regulation?
faqs.a: While many exercises benefit the nervous system, swimming uniquely combines sensory input from water with rhythmic breathing and buoyancy, making it especially effective for somatic regulation.

faqs.q: How often should I swim to experience nervous system benefits?
faqs.a: Consistency matters more than intensity. Even short, regular sessions a few times a week can support nervous system regulation and trauma recovery.
